content:
# Exodus Chapter 24 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~46r-46v (PDF pp. 90-91); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 66-67 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_024_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_024_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE COVENANT RATIFICATION:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 24 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter forms the climactic covenant ratification that seals the Book of the Covenant (20:22-23:33). It moves through three extraordinary scenes: the blood ceremony binding YHWH and Israel (vv. 1-8), the theophanic meal where the elders see God and live (vv. 9-11), and Moses' ascent into the cloud-covered mountain for forty days (vv. 12-18). The chapter is marked by a striking narrative disjunction between vv. 1-2 (the command to ascend) and vv. 3-8 (the altar ceremony at the base), with the actual ascent delayed until v. 9. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 66-67) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script; the versification aligns with the MT throughout, though the LXX introduces notable theological softening at several points, particularly in vv. 10-11.

---

## The Summons to Ascend (vv. 1-2)

**24:1** And to Moses he said, "Come up to YHWH, you and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, and you shall bow down from afar. [fn1]

**24:2** And Moses alone shall draw near to YHWH, and they shall not draw near, and the people shall not come up with him." [fn2]

---

## The Blood of the Covenant (vv. 3-8)

**24:3** And Moses came and recounted to the people all the words of YHWH and all the judgments, and all the people answered with one voice and said, "All the words which YHWH has spoken, we will do." [fn3]

**24:4** And Moses wrote all the words of YHWH, and he rose early in the morning and built an altar at the foot of the mountain and twelve pillars for the twelve tribes of Israel. [fn4]

**24:5** And he sent the young men of the Sons of Israel, and they offered up burnt offerings and they slaughtered peace offerings to YHWH -- bulls. [fn5]

**24:6** And Moses took half of the blood and put it in basins, and half of the blood he threw upon the altar. [fn6]

**24:7** And he took the Book of the Covenant and read in the ears of the people, and they said, "All that YHWH has spoken we will do and we will hear." [fn7]

**24:8** And Moses took the blood and threw it upon the people and said, "Behold the blood of the covenant which YHWH has cut with you upon all these words." [fn8]

---

## The Elders See God (vv. 9-11)

**24:9** And Moses went up, and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel. [fn9]

**24:10** And they saw the God of Israel, and under his feet was something like a work of sapphire pavement, and like the very heavens for clearness. [fn10]

**24:11** And against the nobles of the Sons of Israel he did not send his hand, and they beheld God, and they ate and they drank. [fn11]

---

## Moses Ascends for the Stone Tablets (vv. 12-14)

**24:12** And YHWH said to Moses, "Come up to me to the mountain and be there, and I will give to you the tablets of stone and the Torah and the commandment which I have written to instruct them." [fn12]

**24:13** And Moses arose and Joshua his attendant, and Moses went up to the mountain of God. [fn13]

**24:14** And to the elders he said, "Wait for us here until we return to you, and behold, Aaron and Hur are with you; whoever has a matter shall draw near to them." [fn14]

---

## The Glory of YHWH on Mount Sinai (vv. 15-18)

**24:15** And Moses went up to the mountain, and the cloud covered the mountain. [fn15]

**24:16** And the glory of YHWH settled upon Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days; and he called to Moses on the seventh day from the midst of the cloud. [fn16]

**24:17** And the appearance of the glory of YHWH was like a consuming fire on the top of the mountain before the eyes of the Sons of Israel. [fn17]

**24:18** And Moses entered into the midst of the cloud and went up to the mountain, and Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights. [fn18]

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Ve'el-Mosheh amar aleh el-YHWH attah ve'Aharon Nadav va'Avihu veshiv'im miziqnei Yisra'el vehishtachavitem merachoq*: "And to Moses he said, 'Come up to YHWH, you and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel, and you shall bow down from afar'"** -- EMET renders "And to Moses he said, 'Come up to YHWH...and you shall bow down from afar.'" LOGOS renders "And to Moses He said, 'Come up to YHWH...and you shall bow down from afar.'" PNEUMA renders "Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Come up to YHWH...You are to bow down in worship at a distance.'" The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "Then YHWH said to Moses" supplies the subject explicitly; the Hebrew reads *ve'el-Mosheh amar* ("and to Moses he said") with no explicit subject -- the speaker is inferred from context, and the consensus preserves the Hebrew's ambiguity. PNEUMA's "in worship at a distance" adds "in worship" (not in the Hebrew) and substitutes "at a distance" for the more precise *merachoq* ("from afar"). EMET/LOGOS's "from afar" is preferred. The command establishes a graduated hierarchy of access: Moses alone at the summit, the priestly-elder company at a distance, the people at the base -- anticipating the architecture of the tabernacle.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Veniggash Mosheh levaddo el-YHWH vehem lo yiggashu veha'am lo ya'alu immo*: "And Moses alone shall draw near to YHWH, and they shall not draw near, and the people shall not come up with him"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET and LOGOS render "Moses alone shall draw near to YHWH." PNEUMA renders "Moses alone is to approach YHWH." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "draw near" for *veniggash* (niphal of *nagash*) is more precise than PNEUMA's "approach," preserving the cultic overtones of *nagash*. For the final clause, EMET/LOGOS's "shall not come up" (for *lo ya'alu*) is preferred over PNEUMA's "must not come up." The three concentric zones of holiness -- the people at the base, the elders partway up, Moses alone at the summit -- establish the spatial theology that will be literalized in the tabernacle's concentric courts.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Vayyavo Mosheh vayesapper la'am et kol-divrei YHWH ve'et kol-hammishpatim vayya'an kol-ha'am qol echad vayyomeru kol-haddevarim asher-dibber YHWH na'aseh*: "And Moses came and recounted to the people all the words of YHWH and all the judgments, and all the people answered with one voice and said, 'All the words which YHWH has spoken, we will do'"** -- EMET renders "he recounted." LOGOS renders "told." PNEUMA renders "told." The consensus follows EMET -- "recounted" for *vayesapper* (piel of *safar*, "to recount, declare, tell") is more precise than the generic "told." For *hammishpatim*, EMET renders "judgments," LOGOS renders "judgments," PNEUMA renders "ordinances." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "judgments" preserves *mishpatim* as the legal term for case-law decisions. PNEUMA's "ordinances" is acceptable but less distinctive. For *qol echad*, all three render "with one voice" (or equivalent); the consensus preserves this. This is the first of two declarations of obedience -- *na'aseh* alone ("we will do") -- before the fuller *na'aseh venishma* of v. 7.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Vayyikhtov Mosheh et kol-divrei YHWH vayyashkem babboqer vayyiven mizbe'ach tachat hahar ushtem esreh mattsevah lishnem asar shivtei Yisra'el*: "And Moses wrote all the words of YHWH, and he rose early in the morning and built an altar at the foot of the mountain and twelve pillars for the twelve tribes of Israel"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *tachat hahar*, EMET renders "under the mountain," LOGOS renders "at the foot of the mountain," PNEUMA renders "at the foot of the mountain." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA -- "at the foot of" for *tachat* in this context, as the preposition denotes the base of the mountain rather than literally "under" it. EMET's "under" is hyper-literal. For *mattsevah*, all three agree on "pillars" -- the standing stones that serve as covenant witnesses representing each tribe. This is the first explicit mention of Moses as author; the written document becomes the *sefer habberit* ("Book of the Covenant") of v. 7.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Vayyishlach et-na'arei benei Yisra'el vayya'alu olot vayyizbechu zevachim shelamim la-YHWH parim*: "And he sent the young men of the Sons of Israel, and they offered up burnt offerings and they slaughtered peace offerings to YHWH -- bulls"** -- EMET renders "they offered up burnt offerings, and they slaughtered sacrifices -- peace offerings -- to YHWH: bulls." LOGOS renders "they offered burnt offerings and sacrificed peace offerings to YHWH -- bulls." PNEUMA renders "they offered burnt offerings and sacrificed young bulls as fellowship offerings to YHWH." The consensus follows EMET for "offered up" (*vayya'alu*, from *alah*, "to go up" -- the burnt offering ascends in smoke) and simplifies the double phrase *zevachim shelamim* to "peace offerings" rather than retaining EMET's "sacrifices -- peace offerings" (which is redundant since *zevachim* = "sacrifices" and *shelamim* = "peace offerings" function as a compound noun). PNEUMA's "fellowship offerings" is interpretive -- *shelamim* derives from *shalam* ("to be whole, complete"), and "peace offerings" is the standard rendering. PNEUMA also repositions "young bulls" -- the Hebrew places *parim* ("bulls") at the end as a specifier of the sacrificial animals. The *na'arei* ("young men") serve as sacrificial assistants before the Levitical priesthood is established.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Vayyiqqach Mosheh chatsi haddam vayyasem ba'agganot vachatsi haddam zaraq al-hammizbe'ach*: "And Moses took half of the blood and put it in basins, and half of the blood he threw upon the altar"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *zaraq*, EMET and LOGOS render "threw," PNEUMA renders "splashed." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "threw" for *zaraq* (qal of *zaraq*, "to throw, toss, dash") preserves the forceful, ritualized action of the Hebrew. PNEUMA's "splashed" is not inaccurate but diminishes the deliberate force of the verb. The blood is divided into two equal portions for a two-part ceremony: half on the altar (representing YHWH's side of the covenant) and half reserved in basins for the people (v. 8). The symmetry is exact and theologically precise.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Vayyiqqach sefer habberit vayyiqra be'ozney ha'am vayyomeru kol asher-dibber YHWH na'aseh venishma*: "And he took the Book of the Covenant and read in the ears of the people, and they said, 'All that YHWH has spoken we will do and we will hear'"** -- All three translators agree on the central phrase *na'aseh venishma*. EMET renders "we will do and we will hear." LOGOS renders "we will do and we will hear." PNEUMA renders "we will do -- and we will obey." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "we will do and we will hear" -- preserving the literal meaning of *shama* ("to hear") rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "obey." While *shama* encompasses obedience, the literal "hear" preserves the theologically significant sequence: doing before hearing/understanding. The Talmud (Shabbat 88a) notes the extraordinary priority of action over comprehension in this declaration. For *be'ozney ha'am*, EMET renders "in the ears of the people," LOGOS renders "in the ears of the people," PNEUMA renders "in the hearing of the people." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "in the ears of" preserves the anatomical concreteness of the Hebrew *be'ozney*.

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Vayyiqqach Mosheh et-haddam vayyizroq al-ha'am vayyomer hinneh dam-habberit asher karat YHWH immakhem al kol-haddevarim ha'elleh*: "And Moses took the blood and threw it upon the people and said, 'Behold the blood of the covenant which YHWH has cut with you upon all these words'"** -- EMET renders "Behold the blood of the covenant which YHWH has cut with you upon all these words." LOGOS renders "Behold the blood of the covenant which YHWH has cut with you concerning all these words." PNEUMA renders "This is the blood of the covenant that YHWH has made with you on the basis of all these words." The consensus follows EMET. For *hinneh*, "behold" is preserved rather than PNEUMA's "this is" -- *hinneh* is a demonstrative particle of presentation ("look!"), not a copula. For *karat*, "cut" is preserved (EMET/LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "made" -- covenants are "cut" (*karat*) in Hebrew, and the idiom reflects the ancient ceremony of cutting sacrificial animals. For *al*, EMET's "upon" is preferred over LOGOS's "concerning" and PNEUMA's "on the basis of" -- the preposition *al* here denotes the grounds or terms upon which the covenant rests. The phrase *dam habberit* ("blood of the covenant") occurs only here and in Zechariah 9:11 in the Hebrew Bible; it is directly quoted by Jesus at the Last Supper (Matt 26:28; Mark 14:24) and expounded in Hebrews 9:18-20.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Vayya'al Mosheh ve'Aharon Nadav va'Avihu veshiv'im miziqnei Yisra'el*: "And Moses went up, and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel"** -- All three translators agree. The actual ascent fulfills the command of v. 1. The party of seventy-four (Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy elders) now ascends the mountain.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Vayyir'u et Elohei Yisra'el vetachat raglav kema'aseh livnat hassappir uke'etsem hashamayim latohar*: "And they saw the God of Israel, and under his feet was something like a work of sapphire pavement, and like the very heavens for clearness"** -- EMET renders "like a work of the sapphire stone." LOGOS renders "something like a work of sapphire pavement." PNEUMA renders "something like a pavement of sapphire." The consensus follows LOGOS's "something like a work of sapphire pavement" as the best balance. The key term *livnat hassappir* is a construct chain involving the hapax legomenon *livnah*. The word may derive from *lavan* ("white, brick") -- either "brickwork/pavement of sapphire" or "brilliance of sapphire." The consensus renders *livnat* as "pavement" (following the sense of "brickwork"), since the LXX supports this reading with *plinthon* ("brick/tile"). "Sapphire" (*sappir*) in the ancient world was lapis lazuli, the deep blue stone. For *uke'etsem hashamayim latohar*, all three translators converge: "like the very heavens for clearness" (EMET/LOGOS) or "as clear and pure as the sky itself" (PNEUMA). The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- *etsem* ("bone, substance, essence") is rendered "the very," and *latohar* ("for clearness/purity") is preserved. The LXX notably softens this verse, reading "they saw the place where the God of Israel stood" (*ton topon hou histekei ho theos tou Israel*) rather than directly "they saw the God of Israel" -- a theological deflection to protect divine transcendence.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Ve'el-atsilei benei Yisra'el lo shalach yado vayyechezu et-ha'Elohim vayyokhelu vayyishtu*: "And against the nobles of the Sons of Israel he did not send his hand, and they beheld God, and they ate and they drank"** -- EMET renders "he did not send his hand." LOGOS renders "He did not stretch out His hand." PNEUMA renders "God did not raise his hand." The consensus follows EMET -- "send his hand" for *lo shalach yado* (literally "he did not send his hand") preserves the exact Hebrew idiom. LOGOS's "stretch out" imports the phrasing from other passages (e.g., Exod 3:20), and PNEUMA's "raise" is inaccurate. For *atsilei*, all three agree on "nobles" -- from *atsal* ("to set aside, reserve"); a different word from *ziqnei* ("elders") in vv. 1, 9. For *vayyechezu*, all three render "beheld" -- the shift from *ra'ah* ("to see," v. 10) to *chazah* ("to behold, envision," v. 11) signals a move from direct seeing to prophetic, contemplative beholding. The juxtaposition of divine vision and communal eating is extraordinary -- a covenant meal in the divine presence, where the elders survive what should have been lethal (cf. 33:20).

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Vayyomer YHWH el-Mosheh aleh elai haharah vehyeh-sham ve'ettenah lekha et-luchot ha'even vehatorah vehamitsvah asher katavti lehorotam*: "And YHWH said to Moses, 'Come up to me to the mountain and be there, and I will give to you the tablets of stone and the Torah and the commandment which I have written to instruct them'"** -- EMET renders "the tablets of stone and the Torah and the commandment." LOGOS renders "the tablets of stone and the law and the commandment." PNEUMA renders "the stone tablets -- the law and the commandment." The consensus follows EMET -- "the Torah" for *hatorah* rather than LOGOS/PNEUMA's "the law," because *torah* (from *yarah*, "to instruct, direct") means "instruction" or "teaching" more than "law," and the English word "Torah" preserves this fuller semantic range. The purpose clause *lehorotam* ("to instruct them") shares the same root as *torah*, creating an etymological link that is lost if *torah* is rendered merely as "law." PNEUMA's dash before "the law" makes *hatorah vehamitsvah* appositional to *luchot ha'even*, which is one possible reading but not the only one -- the Hebrew syntax leaves the relationship ambiguous, and the consensus preserves the conjunctive structure. For *katavti*, "I have written" -- God himself is the author of the stone tablets, unlike the Book of the Covenant which Moses wrote (v. 4).

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Vayyaqom Mosheh viYehoshua mesharto vayya'al Mosheh el-har ha'Elohim*: "And Moses arose and Joshua his attendant, and Moses went up to the mountain of God"** -- EMET renders "Joshua his minister." LOGOS renders "Joshua his attendant." PNEUMA renders "Joshua his assistant." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "attendant" for *mesharto* (piel participle of *sharat*, "to minister, serve") is preferred over EMET's "minister" (which carries modern ecclesiastical overtones) and PNEUMA's "assistant" (which is too informal). The *mesharet* serves voluntarily in a position of honor -- it is the language of apprenticeship, not servitude. Joshua's presence is mentioned without explanation of how far he ascends; he reappears in 32:17 waiting on the mountain when Moses descends.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Ve'el-hazzeqenim amar shevu-lanu vazeh ad asher-nashuv aleykhem vehinneh Aharon veChur immakhem mi-ba'al devarim yiggash alehem*: "And to the elders he said, 'Wait for us here until we return to you, and behold, Aaron and Hur are with you; whoever has a matter shall draw near to them'"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *shevu*, EMET renders "wait," LOGOS renders "wait," PNEUMA renders "wait" -- all following the contextual sense of the qal imperative of *yashav* ("to sit, remain, wait"). For *ba'al devarim*, EMET renders "whoever has a matter," LOGOS renders "whoever has a matter," PNEUMA renders "whoever has a dispute." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "a matter" for *devarim* preserves the broader Hebrew term (which can mean "words, things, matters, legal cases") rather than PNEUMA's narrower "dispute." The appointment of Aaron and Hur as interim leaders during Moses' absence is the last mention of Hur in the Exodus narrative; he disappears entirely from the golden calf episode (ch. 32).

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Vayya'al Mosheh el-hahar vayekhas he'anan et-hahar*: "And Moses went up to the mountain, and the cloud covered the mountain"** -- All three translators agree. The theophanic cloud both reveals and conceals YHWH's presence. The definite article (*he'anan*, "the cloud") indicates the specific cloud of divine presence already known from the Exodus narrative (cf. 13:21-22; 19:9, 16). PNEUMA splits this verse at 15a/15b and merges the content of vv. 16-17 into its 15b-16 numbering -- the consensus follows the standard MT versification throughout.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Vayyishkon kevod-YHWH al-har Sinai vaykhassehu he'anan sheshet yamim vayyiqra el-Mosheh bayyom hashevi'i mittokh he'anan*: "And the glory of YHWH settled upon Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days; and he called to Moses on the seventh day from the midst of the cloud"** -- All three translators agree on "settled" for *vayyishkon* (qal of *shakan*, "to dwell, settle, tabernacle"). This verb is theologically critical: the root gives rise to *mishkan* ("tabernacle") and the later concept of *shekhinah* ("divine indwelling presence"). YHWH's glory "tabernacles" on the mountain, anticipating the tabernacle that will be commanded in the following chapters. The six-day covering followed by the seventh-day summons recapitulates the creation pattern, suggesting that the tabernacle instructions Moses will receive are a new creation. The LXX diverges notably, reading *katebe* ("descended") where the MT has *vayyishkon* ("settled/dwelt") -- a significant difference in theological metaphor.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Umar'eh kevod YHWH ke'esh okhelet berosh hahar le'einei benei Yisra'el*: "And the appearance of the glory of YHWH was like a consuming fire on the top of the mountain before the eyes of the Sons of Israel"** -- All three translators agree. For *ke'esh okhelet*, "like a consuming fire" -- the participle *okhelet* (from *akhal*, "to eat, devour") makes the fire alive; it does not merely burn but devours. For *berosh*, EMET renders "on the head of," LOGOS renders "on the top of," PNEUMA renders "on top of." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "on the top of" for *berosh* (literally "on the head of"), which is the standard English idiom for a mountain summit. The LXX renders *esh okhelet* as *pur phlegon* ("blazing fire"), replacing the Hebrew's devouring-eating metaphor with a more conventional description.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Vayyavo Mosheh betokh he'anan vayya'al el-hahar vayehi Mosheh bahar arba'im yom ve'arba'im lailah*: "And Moses entered into the midst of the cloud and went up to the mountain, and Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights"** -- EMET renders "Moses came into the midst of the cloud and he went up to the mountain." LOGOS renders "Moses entered into the midst of the cloud and went up to the mountain." PNEUMA renders "Moses entered the midst of the cloud as he went up the mountain." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "entered" for *vayyavo* is preferred over EMET's "came" because Moses is moving into the cloud rather than arriving from it. PNEUMA's "as he went up" conflates two sequential actions into one simultaneous action; the Hebrew presents them sequentially with consecutive *vav* constructions. The forty days and forty nights (*arba'im yom ve'arba'im lailah*) is a formulaic period of divine encounter (cf. Elijah in 1 Kgs 19:8; Jesus in Matt 4:2), during which Moses receives the tabernacle instructions (chs. 25-31). PNEUMA presents a doubled v. 18, apparently conflating material from vv. 15 and 18 -- the consensus follows the standard MT versification.

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 24 is the hinge of the Pentateuch's covenant narrative -- the chapter that transforms spoken law into binding relationship sealed in blood. It bridges the Book of the Covenant (20:22-23:33) and the tabernacle instructions (25:1-31:18), narrating the formal ratification of the covenant whose terms have just been stipulated while simultaneously preparing for the next divine disclosure by moving Moses onto the mountain for forty days.

The chapter divides into four movements: (1) The divine summons establishing the graduated hierarchy of access (vv. 1-2). (2) The covenant ratification proper, comprising the people's verbal assent, Moses' writing, the sacrificial offerings, the blood ritual, the public reading, and the climactic *na'aseh venishma* (vv. 3-8). (3) The theophanic ascent of the seventy-four, who behold the God of Israel and share a covenant meal in his presence (vv. 9-11). (4) Moses' further ascent, alone, into the cloud of glory, where he will remain for forty days (vv. 12-18).

The chapter's most striking structural feature is the apparent tension in its chronology. Verse 1 ("Come up to YHWH") seems to precede the covenant ceremony of vv. 3-8, yet the ascent does not occur until vv. 9-11. The canonical arrangement traces a progressive movement upward -- from the people at the base, through the elders on the mountain, to Moses alone in the cloud -- enacting spatially the theological principle of mediated access to God.

### Key Decisions

**"Recounted" for *vayesapper* (v. 3):** The piel of *safar* ("to recount, declare, tell") is rendered "recounted" rather than the generic "told" (LOGOS/PNEUMA), preserving the verb's connotation of careful, complete narration -- Moses transmits the covenant terms in full.

**"Judgments" for *hammishpatim* (v. 3):** Preserved rather than PNEUMA's "ordinances" because *mishpatim* is the technical term for case-law decisions, derived from *shaphat* ("to judge").

**"Threw" for *zaraq* (vv. 6, 8):** Preserved over PNEUMA's "splashed." The verb denotes forceful, ritualized casting of blood -- a deliberate, solemn act of covenantal blood-application, not gentle sprinkling.

**"We will do and we will hear" for *na'aseh venishma* (v. 7):** The literal "hear" is preserved for *nishma* rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "obey." The theological significance of the sequence -- doing before hearing/understanding -- is foundational in Jewish theology (Talmud, Shabbat 88a).

**"Sapphire pavement" for *livnat hassappir* (v. 10):** The hapax *livnah* is rendered "pavement," supported by the LXX's *plinthos* ("brick/tile") and the possible connection to *levenah* ("brick") -- with its allusion to Israel's brick-making slavery in Egypt transformed into heavenly splendor. The *sappir* is ancient lapis lazuli, not the modern sapphire.

**"Send his hand" for *shalach yado* (v. 11):** Preserved over LOGOS's "stretch out" and PNEUMA's "raise" as the exact Hebrew idiom -- God did not send forth his destructive power against the nobles.

**"The Torah" for *hatorah* (v. 12):** Preserved rather than "the law" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) because *torah* (from *yarah*, "to instruct") means "instruction, teaching" more than juridical "law," and the English word "Torah" preserves this fuller semantic range.

**"Attendant" for *mesharto* (v. 13):** Preferred over EMET's "minister" (too ecclesiastical in modern English) and PNEUMA's "assistant" (too informal). The *mesharet* serves in a position of honored apprenticeship.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 24 (pp. 66-67) preserves several notable divergences from the MT: (1) In v. 10, the LXX inserts *ton topon* ("the place"), reading "they saw the place where the God of Israel stood" rather than directly "they saw the God of Israel" -- the most significant theological divergence in the chapter, softening the anthropomorphism to protect divine transcendence. (2) In v. 11, the LXX reads *ou diephonesen oudeis* ("not one disagreed/perished") for *lo shalach yado* ("he did not send his hand"), replacing the anthropomorphic idiom with a more abstract statement. (3) In v. 16, the LXX uses *katebe* ("descended") where the MT has *vayyishkon* ("settled/dwelt"), substituting a spatial verb for a relational-theological one rooted in *shakan*. (4) In v. 6, the LXX renders *agganot* ("basins") as *krateras* ("mixing bowls"), a Greek cultural equivalent. (5) In v. 10, the LXX renders *livnat hassappir* as *plinthon sappheirou* ("a brick/tile of sapphire"), taking *livnah* as "brick" -- a reading that may preserve the allusion to the bricks (*levenim*) of Egyptian slavery (Exod 1:14; 5:7-8), now transformed into the pavement of divine glory. (6) In v. 17, the LXX renders *esh okhelet* as *pur phlegon* ("blazing fire"), replacing the Hebrew's devouring-eating metaphor with conventional fire imagery.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
